Hey, while you think you're in a certain business, understand this. First and foremost, you're in the people business. While the manufacturers might be pushing on you product awareness and product knowledge, because they believe the weakness of the sales force of the products you represent might be product. But, in reality, the weakness of all salespeople starts with people. The manufacturer thinks, hey, if our salespeople understood the product and how it worked and the features and benefits, they'd sell more of it. While this is true, that you need to know products and product knowledge and features and benefits, don't forget, people buy products. People are the life. You want to know about people first. It's vital that salespeople understand people first, products second. That doesn't mean products aren't important. They're very important. Features and benefits, very important, but people need to be first. You know, I've known salespeople who understood every in and out of the product, every detail, but couldn't close the door of a refrigerator. Why? Because these were product experts and hadn't become people experts. Look, you've got to realize this. You're in the people business no matter what job your job is. You could be selling products on the internet. You are still in the people business. Your communication still needs to relate with people. So it's product second people first. Okay, so certainly you need product knowledge. You have to know the features. You have to know the benefits. You have to know how your product compares to others, but first and foremost, most importantly, you need to understand people. Otherwise, you'll never get your product or its benefits to them. See most salespeople I meet spend way too much time selling the product and forget that selling is 80% people, 20% product. The reality is this, people buy inferior products every minute of every day. That's right. People buy inferior products every day. Why is this? Because people buy for reasons other than just the benefits of the product. They even buy for other reasons than what they get out of the product. See a person stops in a convenience store after work. I use this example in another segment. They buy a carton of milk. Is this the best milk they can possibly drink? Is this the best price in town? That person doesn't know and probably doesn't care what's in the carton, what makes up the ingredients. It's the convenience in this case that they're buying to provide their home and their family and get there as quickly as possible. A shoe shine guy in a very busy airport doesn't understand that it's not price or even the quality of the shoe shine that keeps people from stopping in his booth. He doesn't realize this because he thinks he's shining shoes. He believes price. If I do it to $5, if I do it for $4, if I do it for $3, that's not the problem. The reason the businessman doesn't stop for a shoe shine in O'Hare Airport is he's concerned about missing his connecting flight. See if the shoe shine person understood people and not just the product, the shine and the money, he would advertise something like this. 60 second shines for free. And then the guy could stop. I could stop running through O'Hare. I got 60 seconds. I can do that. Give me a quick buff. Get me out of here. I'll probably hit him with 20 bucks just because he did it so quick. See this individual, if he truly understood people, he would know how to market, he would know how to promote, he would know how to close, and he would know how actually to deliver a service that people want to pay for. You have to get that right button, that right thing to close the sale. You have to know that you're in the people business in order to ever identify the button. I live in Los Angeles. My wife and I buy gas from a guy not far from us on Sunset Bullet Board. It's a little further than two or three other stations. We go there to fill the car up. He greets us by name, cleans our windshields for free, gives us both a bottle of water for free, all right? Knows my dogs by their names. Do you think I'm going there for the greater quality of the fuel? Do you think I care what he's charging me? Not really, you know? If I have a choice to go into four stations, where do you think I'm stopping? Is he in the people business or the gas business? See this owner understands that he's not in the fuel business. He is in the people business. So what is important to your buyer? What do they need? What is their ideal scene? For them ideal scene means the perfect scene. What is it they're trying to accomplish with a purchase? What is it they really makes them feel good? If they could get everything they wanted, what would that thing be? These are questions that will let you know how to sell them. This puts you back into the people business and not just in the product business. Hey, when a buyer goes out looking for a product, he doesn't care how much you know about the product. He doesn't care how smart you are. He cares about what? Himself, his time, his money, and that he does the right thing for himself. He cares most about himself at this time and you and your product, hey, they're way down on the list. That's the truth about people. You're selling process. Your approach, how you speak to people needs to be people focused on what they want, not just what your organization wants. And we'll be talking about this in future segments, how to take people and push them into your processes. Okay, remember, you're in the people business, not the business you thought you were in.
